prompt: slides.py backs our internal talk-deck previewer — markdown in, slide dicts out, split on lines that are exactly ---, title lifted from the first heading. Presenters keep asking for the reveal.js-ish niceties and I promised them three for Friday. First, speaker notes: inside a slide, the first line that STARTS with 'Note:' kicks off the notes block, which runs to the end of that slide; whatever follows the colon on that line plus all remaining lines becomes slide['notes'] (surrounding whitespace stripped, inner newlines kept), and none of it stays in the body. A 'Note:' in the middle of a sentence is not a marker. Slides without notes get notes=''. Second, fragments: a body line ending in the literal marker <!-- fragment --> is a build step — strip the marker (and the spaces before it) from the body line, and record slide['fragments'] as a list of (n, text) tuples, numbered from 1 within each slide (every slide restarts at 1), where text is the cleaned line without its indentation. Markers only count in the body — one sitting inside the notes block just stays as note text. No markers means fragments=[]. Third, a table of contents: toc_slide(slides, title='Contents') returns a slide-shaped dict (title/body/notes/fragments, notes empty, no fragments) whose body is '1. First Title\n2. Next Title' ... numbering the TITLED slides consecutively and skipping untitled ones entirely; split_slides(text, toc=True) parses as usual then sticks that generated slide at the front. Splitting, title detection, trimming, dropping empty slides — all of that must behave exactly as it does today. python3 test_slides.py is the contract; the tests stay as they are.
